issues.description: Hover-only visual cue for links. Keyboard users do not get equivalent feedback because there is no matching :focus or :focus-visible style.
Removes the visible focus indicator from links and inputs, making keyboard focus not visible.

Modal can only be dismissed via click handlers; there is no Escape key handler to close the modal. This can impede keyboard-only users from quickly exiting the dialog and risks creating a keyboard trap.
When opening the modal, focus is not moved to the modal or its first focusable control. Keyboard users may continue tabbing through background content, resulting in a non-intuitive focus order.
The modal is opened without saving the trigger element and focus is not returned to it on close. This can cause keyboard users to lose their place after dismissing the dialog.

Modal is shown without moving keyboard focus into the dialog. After opening, focus remains on the triggering element or elsewhere in the page, forcing keyboard users to tab through background content instead of the modal, which disrupts operability and meaning.
Modal is shown without moving keyboard focus into the modal. Keyboard users may remain focused on the underlying page, disrupting operability and meaning.
No Escape key handler to close the modal. Users relying on keyboard may not have a standard way to dismiss the dialog.
Focus is not trapped within the modal while it is open. Tabbing can move focus to background content, reducing operability.
On close, focus is not returned to the element that triggered the modal, which can disorient keyboard users.
Missing "Skip to main content" link to let keyboard users bypass repeated navigation.
issues.recommendation:
Add a skip navigation link as the first focusable element in the body and a corresponding main content landmark. Example: Skip to main content
...
Ensure the skip link is visible when focused (e.g., via :focus-visible).

issues.description: Carousel control is a non-semantic div that is not focusable and has no keyboard event handling or accessible name, so it cannot be operated via keyboard.
issues.recommendation: Use a semantic button with an accessible name, e.g., <button id="btn-prev" type="button" aria-label="Previous slide"></button>. If a div must be used, add role="button", tabindex="0", aria-label="Previous slide", and handle Enter/Space keys to activate.

issues.description: Carousel control is a non-semantic div that is not focusable and has no keyboard event handling or accessible name, so it cannot be operated via keyboard.
issues.recommendation: Use a semantic button with an accessible name, e.g., <button id="btn-next" type="button" aria-label="Next slide"></button>. If a div must be used, add role="button", tabindex="0", aria-label="Next slide", and handle Enter/Space keys to activate.

issues.description: Modal can only be dismissed via click activation of buttons; there is no Escape key handler to close the modal. This can leave keyboard users without a quick, standard way to exit the dialog.

When opening the modal, focus is not programmatically moved into the modal. Keyboard focus likely remains on the trigger link behind the overlay, disrupting logical focus order and potentially obscuring the focused element.
When closing the modal, focus is not returned to the element that triggered it. This can disorient keyboard users by dropping focus to an unpredictable location.
Custom modal container lacks role="dialog", aria-modal, and an accessible name association, so assistive tech cannot identify it as a dialog and focus management is impeded.
issues.recommendation:
Add appropriate ARIA to the modal container and reference a titled element. Also make the dialog container focusable for programmatic focus. Example:

Accessibility Issues

...

issues.description: The previous arrow control has no visible focus styling. If this is an interactive control (e.g., a button), keyboard users need a clear focus indicator. Relying solely on default focus may fail if a non-semantic element is used or if defaults are overridden elsewhere.
The next arrow control has no visible focus styling. If this is an interactive control, it needs a clear keyboard focus indicator.

Slide indicator items are styled as interactive (cursor: pointer) but there is no visible focus indicator. If these LIs are clickable controls, keyboard users need focus styles. Additionally, if LIs are used directly as controls, they must be focusable and keyboard-operable.
Focus outline is removed from buttons, eliminating a visible keyboard focus indicator for users navigating via keyboard.
Hover styling is provided without an equivalent keyboard focus style. Keyboard users won’t get the same visual feedback as mouse users when focusing links, which is a hover-only interaction pattern.

Focus styling relies on a subtle text color change (#BEBECF) against a light background (#E9E9ED), which provides low contrast (~1.5:1) and may not be a sufficiently visible focus indicator for keyboard users.
Buttons inside the modal have their focus outline removed, making keyboard focus not visible.

Prev/Next controls are rendered as zero-size elements using borders to form triangles. When focused via keyboard, the default focus outline is not visible on a 0×0 element, making keyboard focus not visible.
Slide indicators are implemented as <li> elements with only a click handler. <li> is not focusable by default and there is no keyboard event handler, so the indicators are not operable via keyboard.
Custom interactive control is created using a <li> without an ARIA role or accessible name. Assistive technologies will not announce an appropriate role or label for these controls.
Text input has no accessible name/label, so screen reader and keyboard users cannot identify the field.
issues.recommendation:
Associate a visible label with the input. Example:
<label for="name">Name</label>
<input type="text" id="name" name="name"/>

issues.description: Email input is missing a programmatically associated label; the preceding text "Email:" is not linked to the input.
issues.recommendation:
Add an explicit label tied to the input. Example:
<label for="email">Email</label>
<input type="email" id="email" name="email"/>

issues.description: Country input lacks an accessible name/label, making it unclear to keyboard and screen reader users.
issues.recommendation:
Provide a label associated with the input. Example:
<label for="country">Country</label>
<input type="text" id="country" name="country"/>

issues.description: CAPTCHA input has no associated label or accessible name; instructions are not programmatically tied to the field.
issues.recommendation:
Add an id and label (and optionally link instructions with aria-describedby). Example:

Please enter the two words you see below, separated by a space

issues.description: Checkbox has no programmatically associated label; the visible text is not linked to the control, so it lacks an accessible name.
issues.recommendation:
Associate the text with the checkbox using a label. Example:
<input type="checkbox" id="major_cs" name="major_cs"/>
<label for="major_cs">Computer Science</label>

issues.description: Checkbox has no programmatically associated label; the visible text is not linked to the control, so it lacks an accessible name.
issues.recommendation:
Associate the text with the checkbox using a label. Example:
<input type="checkbox" id="major_eng" name="major_eng"/>
<label for="major_eng">Engineering</label>

issues.description: Checkbox has no programmatically associated label; the visible text is not linked to the control, so it lacks an accessible name.
issues.recommendation:
Associate the text with the checkbox using a label. Example:
<input type="checkbox" id="major_econ" name="major_econ"/>
<label for="major_econ">Economics</label>

issues.description: Checkbox has no programmatically associated label; the visible text is not linked to the control, so it lacks an accessible name.
issues.recommendation:
Associate the text with the checkbox using a label. Example:
<input type="checkbox" id="major_phy" name="major_phy"/>
<label for="major_phy">Physics</label>

issues.description: Checkbox has no programmatically associated label; the visible text is not linked to the control, so it lacks an accessible name.
issues.recommendation:
Associate the text with the checkbox using a label. Example:

<label for="major_psy">Psychology</label>

issues.description: Checkbox has no programmatically associated label; the visible text is not linked to the control, so it lacks an accessible name.
issues.recommendation:
Associate the text with the checkbox using a label. Example:

<label for="major_sp">Spanish</label>

issues.description: Icon-only/ambiguous-text button lacks a descriptive accessible name; "x" may not be announced meaningfully by assistive tech.
issues.recommendation:
Provide an accessible name. Example:
<button type="button" id="modalClose" aria-label="Close">×</button>

issues.description: Dynamic error message is shown without moving keyboard focus, so keyboard users may remain on the submit control and miss the error. Dynamically inserted content is not receiving focus management.
issues.recommendation:
After showing the error, move focus to the error container and ensure it is focusable. For example:

$('#error')
  .attr('tabindex','-1')
  .html(heading)
  .append(errorMsg)
  .show()
  .focus();

Optionally, return focus to the triggering control after the user addresses errors.

issues.description: Slide indicators are implemented as <li> elements with a mouse-only click handler. The <li> is not focusable and there is no keyboard event handler, so keyboard users cannot activate these controls.
Custom interactive list items lack an explicit role, focusability, and an accessible name. If these are styled as dots with no text, assistive technologies won't announce a name or purpose.

Slide indicators are implemented as <li> elements with a mouse click handler only. <li> is not focusable by default and there is no keyboard event handler, so keyboard users cannot operate the carousel indicators.
Custom interactive control has no explicit role and no accessible name. Screen reader users will not know it is actionable or what it does.
Clickable <li> is not focusable and has only a mouse click handler, so the slide indicators are not operable via keyboard.
Custom interactive element (<li>) lacks an ARIA role and accessible name, so assistive technologies won’t announce it as an interactive control or what it does.

The submenu can be closed by mouse click outside only; there is no keyboard-equivalent action (e.g., Escape key) to dismiss the open submenu. Keyboard-only users cannot explicitly close the submenu without moving focus to another menu item.

Submenu close behavior is tied to mouseout only and lacks a keyboard equivalent when focus leaves the menu. Keyboard users who tab away may leave the submenu open with no analogous automatic close behavior.
Submenu is opened only on mouseenter with no keyboard equivalent, preventing keyboard users from accessing submenus.
issues.recommendation:
Add keyboard equivalents to open the submenu (on focus and on Enter/Space). Example:
$("#menu > li > a")
  .on('focus', function(){ clearTimeout(timer); closeSubMenu(); $(this).siblings('ul').show(); })
  .on('keydown', function(e){ if (e.key === 'Enter' || e.key === ' ') { e.preventDefault(); clearTimeout(timer); $(this).siblings('ul').show(); } });

issues.description: Submenu is closed only on mouseout with a timer; there is no keyboard equivalent to close when focus leaves the menu or to dismiss via keyboard.
Clicking outside closes the submenu, but there is no keyboard method (e.g., Escape key) to dismiss it.
issues.recommendation:
Add an Escape key handler to dismiss the submenu via keyboard. Example:
document.addEventListener('keydown', function(e){ if (e.key === 'Escape') { closeSubMenu(); } });

issues.description: Slide indicators are implemented as <li> elements that only respond to mouse click and are not focusable. Keyboard users cannot reach or activate them, violating keyboard operability.
A non-semantic <li> is being used as an interactive control without an accessible role or name. Assistive technologies won’t announce it as an interactive element or convey its purpose/state.
Hides the currently visible slide without managing keyboard focus. If focus is on an element within the hidden slide, focus may be lost or left on hidden content, disrupting keyboard navigation.
Only CSS classes are toggled to show/hide slides; ARIA state is not updated. Assistive technologies (including screen reader users navigating by keyboard) are not informed which slide is visible.
Slide indicator state is updated via CSS class only. There is no ARIA state to convey which indicator is selected, which can hinder keyboard users relying on assistive tech.
Image used for CAPTCHA is missing an alt attribute, so it has no text alternative for screen reader users.
issues.recommendation: Add an alt attribute that describes the purpose of the image (do not include the CAPTCHA text itself) and provide an alternative non-visual option. Example:

issues.recommendation: Add a descriptive alt attribute, e.g.: . If the image is purely decorative and the caption conveys the full message, use alt="".

issues.description: Text input has no associated label or accessible name.
issues.recommendation: Add a visible label associated via for/id or wrap the input inside a label, e.g.: <label for="name">Name</label> <input id="name" type="text" name="name"/>

issues.description: Missing autocomplete attribute for user name field.
issues.recommendation: Add an appropriate autocomplete token, e.g.: <input id="name" type="text" name="name" autocomplete="name"/>

issues.description: Email input has no associated label or accessible name.
issues.recommendation: Add a visible label associated via for/id or wrap the input inside a label, e.g.: <label for="email">Email</label> <input id="email" type="text" name="email"/>

issues.description: Required field indicated only visually (via surrounding markup) without required/aria-required.
issues.recommendation: Add the required attribute or aria-required="true", e.g.: <input id="email" type="text" name="email" required/>

issues.description: Missing autocomplete attribute for email field.
issues.recommendation: Add autocomplete="email", e.g.: <input id="email" type="text" name="email" autocomplete="email"/>

issues.description: Country input has no associated label or accessible name.
issues.recommendation: Add a visible label associated via for/id or wrap the input inside a label, e.g.: <label for="country">Country</label> <input id="country" type="text" name="country"/>

issues.description: Missing autocomplete attribute for country field.
issues.recommendation: Add autocomplete="country-name", e.g.: <input id="country" type="text" name="country" autocomplete="country-name"/>

issues.recommendation: Associate a label using for/id or wrap the input, e.g.: <label for="major_cs">Computer Science</label> <input id="major_cs" type="checkbox" name="major_cs"/>

issues.recommendation: Add a visible label associated via for/id, e.g.: <label for="captcha">CAPTCHA response</label> <input id="captcha" type="text" name="captcha"/>

issues.description: Helper instruction text is not programmatically associated with the input.
issues.recommendation:
Give the instruction

an id and reference it with aria-describedby on the input, e.g.:

Please enter the two words you see below, separated by a space

issues.recommendation: Add the required attribute or aria-required="true" to the corresponding input elements and include a visible text indicator in the label (not just color). Example:

issues.description: Submission is always prevented and only a generic error message is shown. Inputs are not marked as invalid (aria-invalid) and no programmatic association (aria-describedby) is made between errors and their specific fields.
The input:focus rule removes the visible focus indicator for form fields, making it difficult for keyboard users to identify which input has focus.
Text input has no associated label. The visible text "Name:" is not programmatically associated with the input.

issues.description: Text input has no associated label. The visible text "Email:" is not programmatically associated with the input.

<input id="app-email" type="text" name="email">

issues.description: Field is visually indicated as required (via the surrounding .required styling) but is not programmatically marked as required.
issues.recommendation:
Add the required attribute or aria-required="true". Example:

issues.description: Field is visually indicated as required (via the surrounding .required styling) but is not programmatically marked as required.

issues.description: Email field lacks an autocomplete attribute to identify input purpose.

issues.description: An anchor link is used to trigger an action (open a modal) with its default navigation prevented. This makes a link act like a button, causing a role mismatch for assistive technologies.
Buttons inside the modal have their focus outline removed, hiding the keyboard focus indicator and making keyboard navigation difficult for users who rely on visible focus.
Anchor with href="#" is being used as an interactive control (likely to toggle a submenu) rather than true navigation, resulting in incorrect semantics and potential keyboard behavior mismatches.
issues.recommendation: If this is navigation, provide a real URL: About. If it toggles a submenu, use a button with appropriate ARIA: and toggle aria-expanded via script.

issues.description: Anchor with href="#" used as a control rather than true navigation; this should be a real link or a button if it toggles a submenu.
issues.recommendation: If it navigates, use a real URL: Academics. If it opens a submenu, use: and manage aria-expanded in script.

issues.description: Anchor with href="#" used as an action/control instead of navigation; incorrect semantics for a button-like action.
issues.recommendation: If it navigates, provide a real URL: Admissions. If it controls a submenu, use a button with ARIA: and update aria-expanded via script.

issues.description: Removes the visible focus indicator from interactive elements (links and inputs), making it difficult or impossible for keyboard users to see where focus is.
issues.lineContent: a:hover, a:focus, input:focus { outline: none; border-color: transparent; }

issues.description: Hover-only styling provides visual cues to mouse users but none to keyboard users. Links should have equivalent focus styling so focus is visible when tabbing.
issues.recommendation:
Mirror hover styles for keyboard focus. Example:

#main p a:hover, #main p a:focus-visible { color: #39275b; text-decoration: underline; }

issues.description: Removing the default outline from buttons hides the keyboard focus indicator, making it hard for keyboard users to see which button is focused.
Anchor with href="#" is used as a placeholder/trigger rather than real navigation. If it toggles the submenu, it should be a button; if it’s meant to navigate, it needs a real URL.
issues.recommendation: If it toggles a submenu, use a button with proper ARIA: <button type="button" aria-expanded="false" aria-controls="admissions-submenu">Admissions</button> (and give the submenu id="admissions-submenu"). If it navigates, replace # with the actual URL.

issues.description: Anchor with href="#" is used as a placeholder/trigger rather than real navigation. If it toggles the submenu, it should be a button; if it’s meant to navigate, it needs a real URL.
issues.recommendation: If it toggles a submenu, use a button with proper ARIA: <button type="button" aria-expanded="false" aria-controls="visitors-submenu">Visitors</button> (and give the submenu id="visitors-submenu"). If it navigates, replace # with the actual URL.

issues.description: Div used as a control is not keyboard focusable and lacks keyboard interaction support. Users cannot operate it via keyboard.
issues.recommendation: Use a semantic button: <button type="button" id="btn-prev" aria-label="Previous slide"></button>. If you must keep a div, add role="button" tabindex="0" and handle Enter/Space in keydown.

issues.description: Control has no accessible name; screen readers won’t announce its purpose.
issues.recommendation: Provide an accessible name, e.g., use a button with aria-label: <button type="button" id="btn-prev" aria-label="Previous slide"></button>.

issues.description: Div used as a control is not keyboard focusable and lacks keyboard interaction support. Users cannot operate it via keyboard.
issues.recommendation: Use a semantic button: <button type="button" id="btn-next" aria-label="Next slide"></button>. If you must keep a div, add role="button" tabindex="0" and handle Enter/Space in keydown.

issues.description: Control has no accessible name; screen readers won’t announce its purpose.
issues.recommendation: Provide an accessible name, e.g., use a button with aria-label: <button type="button" id="btn-next" aria-label="Next slide"></button>.

issues.description: Slide indicators are implemented as <li> elements with only a click handler, making them mouse-dependent and not keyboard accessible.
issues.recommendation: Use native buttons (preferred) or add keyboard support and proper semantics. Example (preferred): var lentil = $('<button type=\'button\' class=\'lentil\'>').attr('data-slide', i).on('click', function() { showSlide($(this).data('slide')); }); Or if keeping <li>: var lentil = $('<li tabindex=\'0\' role=\'button\'>').attr('data-slide', i).on('keydown', function(e){ if(e.key === 'Enter' || e.key === ' ') { e.preventDefault(); $(this).click(); } });

issues.description: Interactive slide indicator is created as an empty <li> without a button role or accessible name, so assistive technologies will not recognize it as a control and it has no accessible name.
issues.recommendation: Use a native <button> with an accessible name, or add role and aria-label/visually hidden text. Example (preferred): var lentil = $('<button type=\'button\' class=\'lentil\'>').attr('data-slide', i).attr('aria-label', 'Go to slide ' + (i+1)).on('click', function(){ showSlide($(this).data('slide')); }); If keeping <li>: $('<li tabindex=\'0\' role=\'button\' aria-label=\'Go to slide ' + (i+1) + '\'>')...

issues.description: An anchor link is being used to trigger an action (open a modal) and its default navigation is prevented. This uses a link as a button, which is a semantic misuse.

The close button uses an ambiguous single character ('x') as its label. This functions like an icon-only button without a clear accessible name, so screen readers will announce 'x' rather than a descriptive purpose.
issues.recommendation: Provide a descriptive accessible name. Preferred: use visible text: <button type="button" id="modalClose">Close</button>. If keeping the visual 'x', add an accessible name that includes the visible text to satisfy Label in Name: <button type="button" id="modalClose" aria-label="Close x">x</button>.

issues.description: An anchor link is being used to perform an action (open a modal) by preventing default navigation. This makes a control that behaves like a button but is exposed as a link to assistive technologies, causing a role mismatch and suboptimal keyboard behavior (Space key won't activate).
issues.recommendation: Use a <button> for actions instead of an <a>. Example: HTML: <button id="openCheatsheet">Cheatsheet</button>. JS: $('#openCheatsheet').on('click', function() { showModal(); }); If you must keep an <a>, add role="button" and a keydown handler for Space/Enter, but using a real <button> is recommended.

issues.description: Styled div used as a heading instead of a semantic heading element, so the heading is not programmatically determinable for assistive technologies.
issues.recommendation:
Use a proper heading element that fits the document hierarchy, for example:

Welcome!

. Ensure there is only one h1 on the page and follow sequential heading levels.

issues.description: Heading text is generic and does not describe the topic or purpose of the section when read in isolation.
issues.recommendation:
Use descriptive heading text, for example:

About Accessible University

.

issues.description: Styled div used as a heading instead of a semantic heading element, so the heading is not programmatically determinable for assistive technologies.
issues.recommendation:
Use a proper heading element that fits the document hierarchy, for example:

Bienvenido!

. Ensure there is only one h1 on the page and follow sequential heading levels.

issues.description: Heading text is generic and does not describe the topic or purpose of the section when read in isolation.
issues.recommendation:
Use descriptive heading text, for example:

Bienvenido a Accessible University

.

issues.description: A styled div is used to present a heading, but it is not marked up with a semantic heading element. Screen readers will not recognize it as a heading, and the heading structure is not programmatically determinable.
issues.recommendation:
Replace the styled div with a proper heading element that reflects the content hierarchy, for example:

Accessibility Issues

issues.description: A styled div is used to present a section heading, but it's not a semantic heading element. Screen readers cannot navigate it as a heading, and the heading hierarchy is not programmatically determinable.
issues.recommendation:
Use a semantic heading element in sequential order, e.g.:

Bienvenido!

. Ensure only one h1 on the page and that subsequent headings follow h2 → h3 → ... based on content structure.

issues.description: A styled div is used as a heading instead of a proper h1–h6 element, preventing assistive technologies from recognizing and navigating the heading.
issues.recommendation:
Replace with a semantic heading element, e.g.:

Can you spot the barriers?

, maintaining sequential heading order according to the page hierarchy.

issues.description: This section title is implemented with a styled div rather than a semantic heading element, breaking the heading structure for users relying on headings for navigation.
issues.recommendation:
Use an appropriate heading element, e.g.:

AU Enrollment Trends

, and ensure overall headings are ordered sequentially under a single page h1.

issues.description: Bold text inside a div is being used as a visual heading but is not marked up as a proper heading. Screen readers will not recognize this as a heading.
issues.recommendation:
Use a semantic heading element and style it with CSS as needed, e.g.:

Desired major(s)

issues.description: Bold text is used to indicate a section heading but is not marked up as a heading, so it is not programmatically determinable as a heading.
issues.recommendation:

issues.description: The menu only handles focus changes (focusout) and does not implement required keyboard interactions. There is no keydown handler to support Arrow Up/Down, Left/Right, Home/End, and Escape per ARIA menu patterns.
Open submenus are not dismissible via the Escape key. The closeSubMenu function is only called on focusout and is not bound to Escape, preventing users from easily closing menus with the keyboard.
Submenu is hidden without updating the trigger element's aria-expanded state. Assistive technologies will not receive correct open/closed state for the menu.
Menu interaction is mouse-only. There are no keyboard handlers (e.g., keydown) to support navigation and activation of menu items per the ARIA menu pattern.

Submenu is shown visually without updating ARIA state on the trigger (aria-expanded) or linking the trigger to the submenu via aria-controls. Assistive technologies will not be informed when the menu opens/closes.
Menu only closes on mouse click. There is no Escape key handler to dismiss an open submenu, which is required for keyboard users.
No keyboard interaction handlers are implemented for the menu (e.g., arrow keys, Enter/Space, Escape, Home/End). This prevents keyboard users from navigating and operating the menu per ARIA APG.
Submenu is shown without updating the trigger's aria-expanded state. Assistive technologies will not know the submenu has opened.
Menu triggers do not advertise that they open submenus (missing aria-haspopup).

Add aria-haspopup="true" to triggers that have submenus. Example:
$('#menu > li:has(ul) > a').attr('aria-haspopup','true');

Submenu is shown without updating ARIA state on the trigger (aria-expanded) or linking the trigger to the submenu (aria-controls). This violates the ARIA menu pattern and SC 4.1.2.
No keyboard interaction is implemented for the menu (no keydown handler). Required interactions like Arrow navigation, Escape to close, Home/End, and Enter/Space activation are missing.

Submenu is shown without updating ARIA state on the trigger (aria-expanded) or establishing an aria-controls relationship. Assistive technology users will not be informed when the menu opens.
Submenu is shown on keyboard focus without updating ARIA state on the trigger (aria-expanded) or aria-controls linkage to the submenu. Screen reader users will not receive state changes.

No keydown handlers are implemented; the menu lacks required keyboard interactions (Arrow keys to navigate/open/close, Enter/Space to activate items, Home/End to jump to first/last). Only focus and mouse events are handled.

Submenu is closed by hiding the element, but the associated trigger's aria-expanded state is not updated. This violates the ARIA menu pattern and can leave assistive technology users with incorrect state information.

. JS (after showing the new slide): $('#carousel-status').text('Slide ' + (index + 1) + ' of ' + slideCount);

issues.description: Hidden/visible state changes are not programmatically conveyed. Non-visible slides likely remain exposed to screen readers, which can result in multiple slides being read or focus landing on hidden content.
issues.recommendation: Toggle aria-hidden to reflect visibility when changing slides. Example: $('.slide').attr('aria-hidden', 'true'); var $current = $('.slide').eq(index); $current.attr('aria-hidden', 'false');

issues.description: Slide indicator updates are purely visual and not programmatically determinable (no aria-current/aria-selected), so assistive technologies won't know which indicator is active or that it changed.
issues.recommendation: Update an ARIA state on the active indicator and optionally announce the change. Example: $('.lentils li').attr('aria-current', 'false'); $('.lentils li').eq(index).attr('aria-current', 'true'); // Optionally also update the live region: $('#carousel-status').text('Slide ' + (index + 1) + ' selected.');

issues.description: Submenu is shown dynamically without updating ARIA relationships/state (aria-expanded on the trigger and aria-hidden on the submenu). This makes the show/hide state not programmatically determinable.
Submenu is shown dynamically without updating the trigger's aria-expanded state. Assistive technologies cannot determine whether the menu item is expanded or collapsed.

The trigger and the dynamically shown submenu are not programmatically associated (missing aria-controls). Screen reader users may not understand the relationship between the focused menu item and the submenu content.
Modal is shown dynamically without moving focus to the modal or another stable element. Screen reader users may remain on the trigger while new content appears elsewhere, causing loss of context.

Closing the modal removes/hides content while focus may be on a button within it, without returning focus to a stable element (e.g., the trigger that opened the modal). This can leave screen reader users stranded.

Top-level menu trigger likely controls a dynamically shown/hidden submenu but does not expose or update state (missing aria-expanded) or associate with the controlled content (missing aria-controls). This prevents assistive technologies from understanding the dynamic show/hide relationship.
issues.recommendation:
Make the trigger a button (or keep as link if it navigates) and add aria-expanded and aria-controls, toggled via script. Give the submenu an id. Example:

// JS toggles aria-expanded and the hidden state when activated.

issues.description: Top-level menu trigger likely controls a dynamically shown/hidden submenu but does not expose or update state (missing aria-expanded) or associate with the controlled content (missing aria-controls). This prevents assistive technologies from understanding the dynamic show/hide relationship.
issues.recommendation:
Add aria-expanded and aria-controls to the trigger and toggle them via script; give the submenu an id. Example:

// JS toggles aria-expanded and the hidden state on activation.

This prevents assistive technologies from understanding the dynamic show/hide relationship.
issues.recommendation:
Add aria-expanded and aria-controls to the trigger and toggle them via script; give the submenu an id. Example:

issues.description: Carousel slide changes are not announced to screen readers. The dynamic update is visible but there is no aria-live/status region to communicate the change, so non-visual users may not know a new slide is shown.
Previous button also triggers a slide change without any announcement. Dynamic content update is not communicated via an aria-live/status region.
issues.recommendation:
Update the same polite status region on previous navigation as well:
$('#carousel-status').text('Slide ' + (currentIndex + 1) + ' of ' + slideCount);

issues.description: Slide indicators (lentils) trigger show/hide of slides but are not programmatically associated with the content they control. Without an aria-controls relationship, assistive technologies do not get the structural connection between the indicator and the slide.

Submenu is dynamically shown without updating accessibility state. The trigger element’s aria-expanded is not set to true and the submenu’s aria-hidden is not cleared, so assistive technologies may not be aware of the change.

Submenu is dynamically hidden without updating accessibility state. The trigger element’s aria-expanded is not set to false and the submenu’s aria-hidden is not set to true, leaving assistive technologies unaware of the change and potentially exposing hidden content.
An error status message is injected without an aria-live region or alert role, so screen reader users may not be notified of the update without moving focus.
A heading-like label is inserted as a generic div, which is not programmatically determinable as a heading. This can break information structure for dynamically added content.

Carousel slides and captions are likely updated dynamically via the previous/next controls, but these visual updates are not announced to screen readers because there is no aria-live region or status message.
issues.recommendation:
Add a live region to announce slide changes. Example:

...
and, when the slide changes, programmatically update #carousel-status with text like "Slide 2 of 4: rooted in its history".

issues.description: Submenu is dynamically shown without updating the controlling trigger’s aria-expanded state or associating the trigger with the submenu via aria-controls. Screen reader users won’t be informed of the open/closed state or relationship.
Submenu is hidden after a delay without updating the controlling trigger’s aria-expanded state to false. The state change is not conveyed to assistive technologies.

When the top-level menu item receives keyboard focus, the submenu is shown but the trigger’s aria-expanded state and aria-controls relationship are not set, so the dynamic state is not communicated to screen readers.

Modal container used for dynamically displayed content is not identified as a dialog and lacks aria-modal and an accessible name. Screen reader users may not be informed when this dynamic modal appears.
issues.recommendation:
Add role="dialog" and aria-modal="true" and provide an accessible name via aria-labelledby referencing the modal heading. Example:

issues.description: Modal heading is a generic div rather than a semantic heading, which weakens structure for dynamically shown content and makes it harder to programmatically associate as the dialog's title.
issues.recommendation:
Use a semantic heading element and give it an id, then reference it from the dialog via aria-labelledby. Example:

issues.description: Container for dynamically displayed form errors does not use an aria-live region, so screen reader users will not be notified when errors are injected without moving focus.
issues.recommendation: Use a live region for errors. For urgent validation errors, add role="alert" (assertive) and aria-atomic to announce updates:

issues.description: Submenu is shown without updating the trigger's aria-expanded state or associating the trigger with the submenu via aria-controls. Assistive technologies will not know that content was revealed or how it relates to the trigger.

Closing the previously open submenu does not manage focus. If keyboard focus or a screen reader cursor is inside the submenu when it is hidden, the focused content becomes unavailable, causing disorientation.
Auto-closing the submenu after a timeout does not manage focus. If focus is inside the submenu when it hides, users can lose their place.
issues.recommendation: Ensure closeSubMenu moves focus back to the triggering element and updates aria-expanded before hiding, as in the adjusted closeSubMenu example provided for line 18.

issues.description: Setting a global click handler closes the submenu on any click, including clicks inside the menu, and does not manage focus. This can hide content while focus is within it.
No skip navigation link is provided at the top of the page to let keyboard and screen reader users bypass repeated navigation (banner and menus).
issues.recommendation: Add a skip link as the first focusable element in the body and make it visible on focus. Example: Skip to main content. Ensure it is visually shown on focus with CSS, e.g., .skip-link:focus { position: static; }. Also ensure the target exists (see next issue).

issues.description: There is no main content landmark or programmatically focusable target for a skip link or for focus management after navigation.
issues.recommendation: Use a main landmark with a stable ID and make it programmatically focusable so a skip link or router can move focus to it. Replace this with: <main id="main-content" tabindex="-1"> and close with </main> where the div currently ends.

issues.description: Links are styled without underlines, relying solely on color to indicate they are links. This uses color as the only visual differentiator.
issues.recommendation: Provide a non-color visual cue for links. For example: a { text-decoration: underline; } a:focus { text-decoration: underline; }

issues.description: Removes the visible focus indicator for links and inputs, making focus state hard to perceive. This can result in focus indicators lacking sufficient non-text contrast or being absent.
issues.recommendation: Restore a visible, high-contrast focus style. For example: a:focus, input:focus { outline: 2px solid #005fcc; outline-offset: 2px; }

issues.description: On focus, input borders become transparent, removing a key visual focus indicator and potentially failing non-text contrast requirements.
issues.recommendation: Ensure focused inputs have a visible border or box-shadow with sufficient contrast. For example: input:focus { border-color: #005fcc; box-shadow: 0 0 0 2px #005fcc; }

issues.description: Hardcoded text color for links. Contrast against the background cannot be verified from code alone and should be manually checked to meet minimum contrast.
issues.recommendation: Verify the contrast of #555 against the background (4.5:1 for normal text). If insufficient, use a darker color (e.g., #333) or adjust the background. Example: a { color: #333; }

issues.description: Hardcoded hover color for links. Contrast against the background should be manually verified to meet minimum contrast.
issues.recommendation: Verify the contrast of #39275b against the background (4.5:1 for normal text). If insufficient, choose a higher-contrast color. Example: #2a1b48.

issues.description: Links are distinguished from surrounding text by color alone. Removing the underline makes the link rely solely on color to convey that it is a link.
Hardcoded link text color should be verified to meet minimum contrast against all backgrounds where links appear (e.g., white, #E9E9ED).
issues.recommendation:
Verify contrast with a tool and adjust as needed to meet 4.5:1 (or 3:1 for large/bold). Example:

a { color: #333; text-decoration: underline; }

issues.description: Hardcoded background color for a content container requires manual verification that text inside maintains at least 4.5:1 contrast.
issues.recommendation:
Ensure text within #appForm has sufficient contrast against #E9E9ED. Example:

#appForm { background-color: #E9E9ED; color: #222; }

issues.description: UI component border color requires manual verification for non-text contrast (3:1) against adjacent background (#E9E9ED).
issues.recommendation:
Verify border contrast and adjust if needed. Example:

#appForm { border: 2px solid #2b1f47; }

issues.description: Graphical border on the footer requires manual verification for non-text contrast (3:1) against its adjacent background (likely white).
issues.recommendation:
Verify contrast and adjust color or thickness if needed. Example:

#footer { border-top: 3px solid #3a2d5f; }

issues.description: Hardcoded background color used with text in .modalHeading; the text/background combination must be manually verified to meet minimum contrast requirements.
issues.recommendation: Verify the contrast between #2A194C and the heading text using a contrast checker. If it fails, adjust one of the colors (e.g., darken the background or use a brighter text color). Example: .modalHeading { background-color: #24133F; color: #FFFFFF; }

issues.description: Hardcoded text color on .modalHeading; ensure the white text has sufficient contrast against the background (#2A194C), especially for normal-sized text.
issues.recommendation: Test with a color contrast tool and adjust if needed. For example, if contrast is insufficient, either darken the background or choose a text color with higher contrast: .modalHeading { background-color: #2A194C; color: #FDFDFD; }

issues.description: Removes the default focus indicator on buttons within #modalContent. Focus indicators must be visible and have at least 3:1 contrast against adjacent colors.
issues.recommendation: Restore or replace the focus indicator with a high-contrast style. Example: #modalContent button:focus-visible { outline: 2px solid #005fcc; outline-offset: 2px; } or use a contrasting box-shadow.

issues.description: Black text set at the body level may render directly on the dark #2A194C page background (outside the white #content area), which can result in insufficient contrast for normal-sized text.
Link text uses a hardcoded color that should be manually verified to meet minimum contrast against its background (likely white) for normal and large text.
issues.recommendation:
Verify the contrast of #3E0087 on the page background with a contrast checker. If it falls short of 4.5:1 (normal text) or 3:1 (large/bold), choose a darker shade to increase contrast. For example:

a { color: #2F0065; }

issues.description: Hardcoded link text color requires manual contrast verification. It may not meet the 4.5:1 contrast ratio against the footer background for normal text.
Active slide indicator is conveyed only by a background color change. Users who cannot perceive color differences may not be able to identify the active slide.
issues.recommendation: Add a non-color visual differentiator (e.g., increased size, icon, or high-contrast outline). Example: .lentils li { position: relative; } .lentils li.active { background-color: #FF9; outline: 2px solid #000; transform: scale(1.2); }

issues.description: Prev button arrow uses a very light gray that may not meet the 3:1 non-text contrast requirement against the adjacent background. Hardcoded color should be manually verified.
issues.recommendation: Use a darker color that meets at least 3:1 contrast against the background (verify with a contrast checker). Example: border-right: 40px solid #6E6E73;

issues.description: Next button arrow uses a very light gray that may not meet the 3:1 non-text contrast requirement against the adjacent background. Hardcoded color should be manually verified.
issues.recommendation: Use a darker color that meets at least 3:1 contrast against the background (verify with a contrast checker). Example: border-left: 40px solid #6E6E73;

issues.description: Slide indicator (lentil) uses a white fill which may have insufficient non-text contrast against a light background. Hardcoded UI component colors should be manually verified.
issues.recommendation: Ensure the lentil has at least 3:1 contrast against the background, e.g., add a high-contrast border. Example: .lentils li { background-color: #FFF; border: 2px solid #444; }

issues.description: Active lentil uses a pale yellow fill that may not meet the 3:1 non-text contrast requirement against adjacent backgrounds. Hardcoded UI component colors should be manually verified.
issues.recommendation: Increase contrast for the active indicator, e.g., use a darker color or add a high-contrast outline. Example: .lentils li.active { background-color: #FFC107; outline: 2px solid #333; }

issues.description: Required fields are indicated by color alone. Color must not be the only visual means of conveying that a field is required.
issues.recommendation: Add a visible, non-color indicator and programmatic markup. Example: and keep color as supplementary styling (e.g., .req-indicator { color: #39275B; font-weight: bold; }).

issues.description: Hardcoded text color should be manually verified for sufficient contrast against the background.
issues.recommendation: Verify the contrast of #39275B against the actual background using a contrast checker and adjust either the text color or background to meet 4.5:1 (3:1 for large/bold). Example: if on white, darken the purple or choose a higher-contrast shade if it does not meet the ratio.

issues.description: Hardcoded light background color for an error message without an explicit text color. Text over this background must be verified to meet minimum contrast (4.5:1 for normal text).
issues.recommendation: Set an explicit text color with sufficient contrast against the background (and verify with a contrast checker). For example: #errorMsg { background-color: #FFFFCC; color: #000; }

issues.description: Hardcoded border color on an alert-style component should be verified to meet non-text contrast (3:1) against the adjacent background.
issues.recommendation: Ensure the border color contrasts at least 3:1 with the surrounding background; adjust color or thickness if needed. For example: #errorMsg { border: 2px solid #000; } (verify final choice with a contrast tool).

issues.description: Table header uses a very light background color without an explicit text color. Text over this background should be verified to meet the 4.5:1 contrast requirement.
issues.recommendation: Declare a sufficiently contrasting text color or darken the background. For example: table#enrollment th { background-color: #f0ecf9; color: #000; }

issues.description: Buttons inside the modal have their default focus outline removed, which can result in no visible focus indicator. Focus cues are non-text UI indicators and must be clearly visible with sufficient contrast; removing them fails non-text contrast requirements.

Hardcoded text and background colors are used for the heading. Contrast between white text and the #2A194C background must be manually verified to meet 4.5:1 for normal text or 3:1 for large/bold text.
issues.recommendation: Verify the contrast using a tool (axe, Lighthouse, Colour Contrast Analyser). If it does not meet at least 3:1 for large bold text, adjust colors to increase contrast (e.g., darken the background further).

issues.description: Caption text is absolutely positioned over the slide image without a solid background or overlay. Text (#f0f2f5 from the parent .slide) over varying image content can result in insufficient contrast, especially in lighter areas of images.

The prev arrow is a light gray triangular icon overlaid on varying slide content with no solid button background. As a non-text UI control, its contrast against adjacent colors may be insufficient, especially over light areas of images.

Hardcoded pale yellow background for the error container may cause insufficient text contrast if the inherited text color is not dark enough. Text over this background must meet 4.5:1 (3:1 for large/bold) contrast.
Hardcoded border color on the error container requires verification for non-text contrast (border vs adjacent background #FFC) to ensure it is distinguishable (minimum 3:1).
Menu link text is a light gray-blue on a light gray background (#E9E9ED set on lines 18 and 35). This is an obviously low-contrast combination and likely fails the 4.5:1 minimum text contrast.
Focus state sets the link text to very light gray against a light gray background (#E9E9ED). This is likely insufficient text contrast and makes the focus state hard to perceive.
issues.recommendation:
Use a darker focus text color that meets 4.5:1 and/or add a visible focus outline with 3:1 contrast. Example:
#menu a:focus { color: #2B2E34; outline: 2px solid #39275b; outline-offset: 2px; }

issues.description: Menu item left border uses a light gray against a light gray background (#E9E9ED). Component boundary may not meet the 3:1 non-text contrast requirement.
issues.recommendation:
Use a darker border color or increase thickness to achieve at least 3:1. Example:
#menu > li { border-left: 1px solid #666; }

issues.description: Menu item top border is light gray on a light gray background (#E9E9ED), which may be insufficient non-text contrast.
issues.recommendation:
Darken the border to meet 3:1. Example:
#menu > li { border-top: 1px solid #666; }

issues.description: Menu item right border uses a light gray on a light gray background (#E9E9ED). Even at 2px, the color may not meet 3:1 non-text contrast.
issues.recommendation:
Use a darker border color. Example:
#menu > li { border-right: 2px solid #555; }

issues.description: Submenu container border is light gray on a light gray background (#E9E9ED), which may not meet the 3:1 non-text contrast requirement.
issues.recommendation:
Darken the border color. Example:
#menu li ul { border: 1px solid #666; }

issues.description: Submenu container right border uses a light gray on a light gray background (#E9E9ED), which may be insufficient non-text contrast.
issues.recommendation:
Use a darker border color. Example:
#menu li ul { border-right: 2px solid #555; }

issues.description: Submenu item separators are very light gray on a light gray background (#E9E9ED), likely failing the 3:1 non-text contrast requirement.
issues.recommendation:
Use a darker separator and a consistent thickness. Example:
#menu li ul li { border-bottom: 1px solid #666; }

issues.description: The current/selected state is conveyed only by a background color change. There is no additional visual indicator (e.g., text or icon) to denote the current page for users who may not perceive color differences.
Button/link borders use light gray tones that may not provide sufficient non-text contrast against the light background (#E9E9ED), making component boundaries hard to perceive.
Hardcoded text and background colors for nav links may not meet the minimum 4.5:1 contrast ratio for normal text. Requires manual verification.

The focus indicator relies on a subtle background-color change and a box-shadow that may not achieve the required 3:1 contrast against adjacent colors. Requires manual verification of focus indication visibility.
Focus/hover state text color (#2A194C) on background (#e8e3d3) may not meet the 4.5:1 contrast ratio for normal text. Requires manual verification.
